physiologicalParams.name: Height growth velocity
physiologicalParams.typical: 5–9 cm per year during the teen growth spurt
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Steady, on-track growth along the individual curve
physiologicalParams.driver: Adequate energy, protein, calcium, and 8–10 h of sleep

physiologicalParams.name: Bone mineral density
physiologicalParams.typical: Peak accrual years — roughly half of adult bone is built in the teens
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Faster bone mineral gain over months
physiologicalParams.driver: Calcium and vitamin D plus impact sports like basketball and soccer

physiologicalParams.name: Aerobic fitness (estimated VO2max)
physiologicalParams.driver: Regular running-based sport sessions raise heart and lung capacity

physiologicalParams.name: Resting heart rate
physiologicalParams.driver: Repeated aerobic training increases stroke volume

physiologicalParams.name: Muscular strength & coordination
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Noticeably stronger and more coordinated in 6–10 weeks
physiologicalParams.driver: Sport skill practice plus maturing nervous system and adequate protein

physiologicalParams.name: Healthy weight trajectory (BMI-for-age)
physiologicalParams.typical: Within the healthy percentile band for age
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Stays in the healthy band while height and muscle increase
physiologicalParams.driver: Energy intake matched to high daily activity

physiologicalParams.name: Sleep duration & quality
physiologicalParams.typical: 8–10 h per night recommended for teens
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Easier sleep onset and deeper sleep
physiologicalParams.driver: Daytime exercise and limited late-evening sugar and screens

physiologicalParams.name: Focus & mood
physiologicalParams.typical: Varies with meals, sleep, and activity
physiologicalParams.expectedChange: Steadier energy and mood across the school day
physiologicalParams.driver: Regular balanced meals prevent energy dips; exercise supports mood

Trail mix with dried fruit; fresh orange
Spaghetti with lean beef marinara, garden salad with olive-oil dressing, garlic bread

week.highlight: A hearty pasta dinner refuels muscles after an intense basketball practice.

exercise.activity: School PE class + easy evening bike ride

week.highlight: An Asian-inspired day with rice and noodles keeps carbohydrate stores topped up for daily sport.

week.highlight: A Mediterranean day pairs olive-oil-based fats with low-impact swimming to give joints a break.

Apple slices with yogurt dip; small handful of almonds

Salmon and avocado sushi rolls with edamame; orange

Minestrone soup with a whole-grain roll, cheese slice, and apple

week.highlight: A lighter recovery day with an easy walk and stretching lets muscles rebuild after the weekend match.

micronutrients.name: Iron
micronutrients.keySources: Lean beef, chicken, lentils, fortified cereal

nutrition: Consistent energy intake around 2600 kcal supports the teen growth spurt in height and lean mass rather than fat gain.
Roughly 1300 mg of daily calcium with vitamin D drives peak bone mineral accrual during these key building years.
Regular carbohydrate at every meal keeps muscle glycogen full, so sport sessions and school focus don't run out of fuel.
Roughly 35–45 g of daily fiber from whole grains, beans, and fruit supports digestion and steadier blood-sugar curves.
Protein spread across four eating occasions (about 115–135 g per day, comfortably above the 65 g minimum) supplies the amino acids growing muscle needs.
exercise: Five-plus active days per week raise aerobic capacity (VO2max) by roughly 5–10% within 8–12 weeks.
Impact sports like basketball and soccer stimulate bone growth exactly when the skeleton is most responsive.
Mixing sports (ball games, swimming, cycling) builds broad coordination and reduces overuse injuries from one repeated motion.
Regular vigorous play lowers resting heart rate a few beats as the heart pumps more per stroke.
A weekly light recovery day with walking and stretching helps muscles adapt and keeps motivation high.

notes: Teen years are for building — bone, muscle, and habits — so this week focuses on fueling sport and growth, not restricting. Portions can flex up on heavy training days; hunger is usually a reliable guide at this age.
